text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC — ON-STUDY ASSESSMENT NOTE
Cycle 1, Week 4 — Phase I/II Clinical Trial
Investigational EGFR/MET Bispecific Antibody + Anti-PD-L1 Agent

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Yuen is a 64-year-old Chinese-American woman with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0 at diagnosis, now recurrent/metastatic) EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma who presents today for her cycle 1, week 4 on-trial assessment. She is now approximately 4 weeks into her first cycle of investigational therapy on the Phase I/II study evaluating the EGFR/MET bispecific antibody in combination with an anti-PD-L1 agent. She is accompanied today by her daughter, Linda.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Yuen's oncologic history is extensive and is summarized as follows for context:

She was initially diagnosed approximately 2 years ago with Stage IIIB right upper lobe pulmonary adenocarcinoma (T3N2M0 by AJCC 8th edition) on the basis of a 4.8 cm spiculated right upper lobe mass with PET-avid ipsilateral mediastinal nodal involvement at stations 4R and 7. Tissue biopsy confirmed invasive pulmonary adenocarcinoma, acinar-predominant, TTF-1 positive. Molecular profiling at diagnosis revealed an EGFR exon 21 L858R sensitizing mutation (VAF 42%), TP53 R248W co-mutation, STK11/KEAP1 wild-type, no MET amplification, PD-L1 TPS 15%, and low TMB (6.2 mut/Mb) by a 500-gene NGS panel. ALK, ROS1, RET, and NTRK fusions were all negative.

At the time of diagnosis, multidisciplinary tumor board review determined the tumor to be unresectable given multi-station N2 involvement. Concurrent chemoradiation was discussed but declined by the patient due to concern about compounding toxicity in the setting of her underlying GOLD Stage I COPD. After extensive shared decision-making, upfront osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily was initiated as definitive-intent first-line therapy. She achieved a marked partial response — the primary mass shrank from 4.8 cm to a nadir of 1.8 cm at 9 months, and mediastinal lymph nodes normalized in size. She tolerated osimertinib reasonably well, with grade 2 acneiform rash managed with topical clindamycin and oral doxycycline (no dose reduction required), grade 1 paronychia of bilateral first toes, grade 1 diarrhea, and grade 1 dry eye syndrome. At her best response timepoint, her ECOG performance status had improved to 0.

At approximately 14 months on osimertinib, restaging CT chest and MRI brain demonstrated systemic and intracranial progression: the right upper lobe primary mass rebounded to 3.2 cm, new right paratracheal lymphadenopathy appeared, a small right pleural effusion emerged, and two new enhancing brain lesions were identified — a 1.1 cm right frontal lobe lesion and a 0.8 cm right cerebellar lesion. Liquid biopsy (Guardant360 ctDNA panel) revealed acquired EGFR T790M (VAF 1.6%) alongside persistent EGFR L858R and TP53 R248W; no C797S, MET amplification, or ERBB2 amplification was detected in plasma at that time.

She then underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to both brain metastases using the Varian Edge system (right frontal: 20 Gy x 1; right cerebellar: 22 Gy x 1) with good intracranial response on subsequent MRI. She was transitioned to second-line platinum-based chemotherapy with carboplatin (AUC 5) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2) plus bevacizumab (15 mg/kg) every 3 weeks. She completed 4 cycles of induction with confirmed partial systemic response (primary mass 2.4 cm), and then transitioned to maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab. On maintenance, she developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy in bilateral feet (attributed to prior carboplatin; managed with duloxetine 60 mg daily) and grade 1 bevacizumab-related hypertension (amlodipine 5 mg added).

At approximately 6 months of maintenance (roughly 11 months after initiating second-line therapy), repeat restaging imaging documented second systemic progression: the right upper lobe mass enlarged from 2.2 cm to 3.8 cm, new bilateral pulmonary nodules appeared (right lower lobe 0.8 cm, left lower lobe 0.6 cm), and the previously SRS-treated right frontal lesion increased from 0.6 cm to 1.4 cm with surrounding edema. MRI perfusion and spectroscopy were performed to differentiate radiation necrosis from tumor recurrence; elevated rCBV and a high choline:NAA ratio favored true tumor recurrence over necrosis. Maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab was discontinued.

Repeat SRS was delivered to the right frontal recurrent lesion (18 Gy x 1, dose-reduced given prior irradiation of the same volume). The cerebellar lesion remained resolved and did not require retreatment. She was started on a short course of dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily for peritumoral edema.

Prior to trial enrollment, repeat tissue biopsy of the right upper lobe mass was performed for re-profiling. FoundationOne CDx genomic profiling of the fresh FFPE specimen revealed: EGFR L858R (VAF 48%), EGFR T790M (VAF 22%, now higher in tissue), MET high-level amplification (copy number 6.8) as an identified bypass resistance mechanism, RB1 p.Q670* nonsense mutation (VAF 18%, newly detected), ARID1A p.E1814* at higher VAF (24%), CDK4 and CCNE1 low-level amplifications, and TP53 R248W. TMB 8.4 mut/Mb. PD-L1 TPS increased to 28%. No C797S, no fusions detected. The combination of EGFR L858R/T790M co-mutation with high-level MET amplification confirmed the scientific rationale for enrolling on the EGFR/MET bispecific antibody trial.

Ms. Yuen enrolled on the Phase I/II study and received Cycle 1, Day 1 of investigational therapy approximately 4 weeks ago, after completing the dexamethasone taper. Her daughter Linda was present for the consent process and has been a consistent support throughout her care.

Today, Ms. Yuen reports the following:
- Headaches: Markedly improved compared to the period before and immediately after repeat SRS. She rates current headache intensity at 1-2/10. She is no longer taking acetaminophen or any residual steroid for headache relief. She denies any new neurological symptoms, including no vision changes, no motor weakness, no gait instability, no confusion, no seizure activity.
- Dyspnea: She notes a modest but perceptible improvement in her exertional dyspnea compared to her state at second progression. She is able to ambulate from her car to the clinic (approximately 200 meters) without stopping, though she does note mild shortness of breath with faster walking. No dyspnea at rest. No orthopnea.
- Fatigue: She describes grade 1 fatigue — she is tired by mid-afternoon but can manage activities of daily living independently. She is spending time at home reading and watching her favorite historical dramas on TV; she is not housebound.
- Nausea/Appetite: Mild intermittent nausea (grade 1), particularly in the first week following the first infusion. No vomiting. Appetite mildly reduced; she has been eating about 75% of her usual food intake. She has lost approximately 1.5 kg over the past month.
- Infusion-related reaction: She and her daughter describe that during the first infusion (Cycle 1, Day 1), she developed flushing, mild chills, and a sensation of tightening in her chest within the first 20-30 minutes of the infusion. The infusion was paused, she was administered IV diphenhydramine 25 mg and IV methylprednisolone per protocol, and the reaction resolved within 15 minutes. The infusion was restarted at a slower rate without recurrence. Per protocol, pre-medications (diphenhydramine, acetaminophen, and corticosteroid pre-treatment) are now planned for all future infusions. She did not have any recurrent reaction during the Day 15 cycle 1 administration.
- Skin: No significant new rash noted. She has mild residual xerosis from prior osimertinib exposure; using emollient moisturizers. No new acneiform eruption.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild bilateral foot and ankle aching, consistent with residual chemotherapy-related peripheral neuropathy (baseline grade 2 at last assessment). She reports this feels subjectively similar to her baseline — neither clearly better nor worse since starting trial therapy. Duloxetine 60 mg daily continued.
- Bowel/Bladder: No diarrhea or constipation. Urinary function normal. No blood in stool or urine.
- Cough: Mild dry cough, mostly in the morning. She attributes this to her underlying COPD and prior lung disease; no hemoptysis.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Fatigue (grade 1), mild exertional dyspnea, mild headache (improved), mild nausea (grade 1), mild dry cough, reduced appetite, weight loss (~1.5 kg over 4 weeks), peripheral neuropathy (bilateral feet, stable from baseline).
Negative: No fever, chills, or night sweats (beyond the transient infusion reaction). No chest pain or palpitations. No focal neurologic deficits — no new weakness, sensory changes beyond known neuropathy baseline, vision changes, diplopia, or ataxia. No seizure activity. No new headaches beyond residual mild baseline. No hemoptysis. No significant nausea or vomiting at present. No abdominal pain, bloating, or change in bowel habits. No hematuria. No lower extremity edema. No joint pain or swelling. No rash. No oral mucositis.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant — Stage IIIB at initial diagnosis, now recurrent/metastatic with brain involvement (as above)
2. Hypertension — well-controlled on amlodipine and prior antihypertensive (lisinopril per earlier records)
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled, HbA1c 6.9% at last check (6 months ago)
4. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease — GOLD Stage I, mild; managed with as-needed short-acting bronchodilator
5. Prior EGFR TKI-related dermatologic toxicity (grade 2 acneiform rash, resolved)
6. Carboplatin-related peripheral neuropathy (grade 2, ongoing, stable on duloxetine)
7. Prior brain metastases, status post SRS x2 (right frontal lobe; right cerebellar hemisphere treated with single SRS course, now resolved)

SOCIAL HISTORY
Ms. Yuen is a retired high school chemistry teacher. She resides in San Francisco, CA, with her husband. She has a 20 pack-year smoking history; she quit 14 years ago (at the time of diagnosis she had quit 12 years prior; now approximately 2 years further). She does not drink alcohol regularly. No illicit drug use. She has a strong family support system — her daughter Linda accompanies her to most appointments and is well-informed about her diagnosis and treatment. She reports some financial strain related to copays and reduced retirement income; social work has been involved. She has a good understanding of her diagnosis and actively participates in medical decision-making.

FAMILY HISTORY
Mother: deceased, breast cancer diagnosed in her 60s.
Father: deceased, coronary artery disease.
No known family history of lung cancer. No known EGFR germline mutations.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies (prior infusion reaction to study drug — managed with pre-medications per protocol; this is a recognized study drug reaction, not a true allergy).

MEDICATIONS
1. Investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody (study drug A) — per protocol dosing, IV
2. Investigational anti-PD-L1 agent (study drug B) — per protocol dosing, IV
3. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily (bevacizumab-related hypertension; bevacizumab now discontinued, continue for BP control)
4. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (pre-existing hypertension management)
5. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes)
6. Duloxetine 60 mg orally once daily (chemotherapy-related peripheral neuropathy)
7. Tiotropium bromide inhaler (Spiriva) 18 mcg once daily (COPD)
8. Albuterol MDI as needed (COPD)
9. Emollient moisturizer twice daily (residual xerosis from prior osimertinib)
10. Folic acid 400 mcg orally once daily (retained from prior pemetrexed; to be discontinued per protocol at next visit unless pemetrexed re-introduced)
11. Vitamin B12 supplementation (retained from prior pemetrexed course; to discuss discontinuation)
12. Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed for headache (infrequent use; last taken approximately 10 days ago)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Ms. Yuen is a well-appearing, pleasant Chinese-American woman who appears her stated age. She is seated comfortably in the exam chair. She is alert and fully oriented to person, place, time, and clinical situation. She is in no acute distress. She is accompanied by her daughter Linda, who is attentive and engaged. Weight: 56.2 kg (down from 57.7 kg at last visit, approximately 1.5 kg loss). Height: 158 cm. BMI: 22.5 kg/m2.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg (left arm, seated; well-controlled on current regimen). Heart rate 74 bpm, regular. Respiratory rate 16 breaths/minute. Temperature 36.8°C (oral). Oxygen saturation 95% on room air (baseline for her given underlying COPD; no change from recent prior visits).

ECOG Performance Status: 1 (ambulatory, able to carry out light work and self-care; up and about more than 50% of waking hours).

HEENT: Normocephalic. Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam (deferred today; last formal ophthalmologic exam normal at last assessment). Mild conjunctival pallor bilaterally, not marked. No oral mucositis, no thrush. Mucous membranes moist.

Neck: No lymphadenopathy palpable in the anterior or posterior cervical chains or supraclavicular fossae. Trachea midline. Thyroid not enlarged.

Lungs: Breath sounds reduced in the right upper lobe region compared to the left, with dullness to percussion at the right base (likely residual from prior pleural effusion, though the effusion had resolved on last imaging; may represent subtle atelectasis or pleural thickening). No wheezes audible at rest. No rales or crackles. Mild expiratory prolongation bilaterally, consistent with known COPD. No egophony.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 heard without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. No lower extremity edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. Bowel sounds active and normoactive in all four quadrants. No palpable masses.

Extremities: Mild bilateral lower extremity peripheral neuropathy distribution — reduced light touch and vibratory sensation in a stocking distribution to the mid-calf bilaterally (consistent with known grade 2 carboplatin-related neuropathy). Capillary refill less than 2 seconds bilaterally. No new edema. Fingernails: trace paronychia at bilateral first toes, stable.

Skin: Mild residual xerosis on the face and upper chest, consistent with prior EGFR TKI-related skin changes. No active acneiform rash. No new lesions. No infusion site reactions.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact on focused examination. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities tested (bilateral upper extremities, bilateral lower extremities). Gait steady without ataxia. Coordination intact — finger-nose testing normal bilaterally. Romberg test negative. Reduced pin-prick and light touch sensation in bilateral feet in a stocking distribution (baseline neuropathy).

Lymph Nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or palpable supraclavicular adenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
(Obtained today, per on-study protocol requirements)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.2 x 10^9/L (normal range: 4.5-11.0) — within normal limits
- ANC: 3.1 x 10^9/L — adequate
- Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (low; baseline has been in the 10-11 range given prior chemotherapy; no acute change, grade 1 anemia — stable)
- Hematocrit: 32.5%
- Platelets: 184 x 10^9/L — within normal limits
- MCV: 88 fL — normocytic

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.8 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL; eGFR > 60 mL/min/1.73 m2 — adequate renal function for protocol dosing
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- AST: 28 U/L (upper limit of normal for lab: 35 U/L) — normal
- ALT: 32 U/L (upper limit of normal: 40 U/L) — normal; important to monitor for immune-related hepatotoxicity given anti-PD-L1 component of trial
- Alkaline phosphatase: 72 U/L — normal
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL — normal
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL — low-normal; consistent with ongoing mild nutritional stress and disease burden
- Total protein: 6.8 g/dL
- Glucose: 112 mg/dL (fasting) — borderline; consistent with diabetes; HbA1c pending
- Calcium: 9.1 mg/dL — normal

Thyroid Function (per protocol, immune checkpoint baseline/monitoring):
- TSH: 2.4 mIU/L — normal
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL — normal
(No evidence of thyroid dysfunction from anti-PD-L1 agent at cycle 1, week 4)

Coagulation:
- PT/INR: 1.0 — normal
- aPTT: 28 seconds — normal

Urinalysis (per protocol):
- Urine protein: negative (no proteinuria; bevacizumab has been discontinued but baseline assessment relevant)
- No blood, no glucose spill on dipstick

Cardiac:
- 12-lead ECG obtained per protocol: Normal sinus rhythm, rate 74 bpm. QTc interval: 438 ms (within normal limits; <450 ms for females). No ST changes. No conduction abnormalities. (QTc monitoring protocol for EGFR/MET bispecific agent ongoing.)
- Echocardiogram (obtained at cycle 1 day 1 baseline): LVEF 62% (preserved). No wall motion abnormalities. No significant valvular disease. (Repeat ECHO scheduled at approximately cycle 4 per protocol.)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
(No new imaging obtained today; per protocol, first on-study restaging is scheduled at approximately cycle 3, approximately 6 weeks from treatment initiation — i.e., approximately 2 weeks from today's visit)

Most recent imaging context (for reference):
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis at second progression (approximately 5 weeks prior to today): Right upper lobe mass 3.8 cm, new bilateral pulmonary nodules (RLL 0.8 cm, LLL 0.6 cm), no pleural effusion at that time.
- MRI brain perfusion/spectroscopy (at second progression): Right frontal recurrent lesion 1.4 cm with features favoring tumor recurrence; treated with repeat SRS (18 Gy x 1) approximately 3 weeks prior to trial cycle 1 day 1.
- Dexamethasone taper completed prior to trial enrollment, as required by the protocol.

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain will be obtained at the end of cycle 3 per protocol schedule.

---

ADVERSE EVENT ASSESSMENT (CTCAE v5.0)

1. Infusion-related reaction — Grade 1 (flushing, chills, chest tightening during cycle 1, day 1 infusion; resolved with standard management). No recurrence on day 15. Pre-medications now standardized per protocol. Attribution: Likely related to study drug (EGFR/MET bispecific antibody).

2. Fatigue — Grade 1 (mild, not limiting activities of daily living). Attribution: Possibly related to study treatment; may also represent ongoing disease-related fatigue and residual cumulative toxicity from prior therapies.

3. Nausea — Grade 1 (mild, intermittent, no vomiting). Managed with dietary modification; ondansetron available as needed. Attribution: Possibly related to study treatment.

4. Peripheral neuropathy — Grade 2 (sensory, bilateral feet, stocking distribution; stable from pre-trial baseline, not newly worsened). Attribution: Residual from prior carboplatin exposure. Not attributed to current study drug.

5. Anemia — Grade 1 (Hgb 10.8 g/dL; stable from prior). Attribution: Residual from prior chemotherapy; not actively worsening. Continued monitoring.

6. Weight loss — Approximately 1.5 kg over the 4-week cycle. Grade 1. Monitored; nutritional counseling recommended. Attribution: Multifactorial (disease burden, reduced appetite from mild nausea, deconditioning).

No grade 3 or higher adverse events. No new immune-related adverse events (no hepatitis, no colitis, no pneumonitis, no endocrinopathy at 4-week timepoint). Thyroid function normal. Liver enzymes normal.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Yuen is a 64-year-old woman with EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma, now with MET amplification-driven acquired resistance, presenting for her cycle 1, week 4 on-study assessment on the Phase I/II trial evaluating an investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody in combination with an anti-PD-L1 agent. She completed the first cycle of therapy (day 1 and day 15 dosing) with manageable tolerability. She is now 4 weeks into treatment, and it is too early to formally assess response — first restaging imaging is planned in approximately 2 weeks (end of cycle 3 per protocol).

1. Oncologic status: On active Phase I/II clinical trial. Second progression following osimertinib (1st-line) and carboplatin/pemetrexed/bevacizumab followed by pemetrexed/bevacizumab maintenance (2nd-line). Tumor harbors EGFR L858R, T790M, and high-level MET amplification (CN 6.8) as key resistance drivers — directly targeted by current trial regimen. PD-L1 TPS 28% provides some scientific rationale for the anti-PD-L1 partner agent. Brain metastases: right frontal lesion treated with repeat SRS 3 weeks prior to cycle 1 day 1; right cerebellar lesion previously resolved after initial SRS. No new untreated brain lesions on last MRI (obtained at time of second progression). Brain imaging will be obtained at the cycle 3 restaging time point.

   - Continue study treatment per protocol schedule. Cycle 2, Day 1 to be scheduled.
   - First on-study restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain with gadolinium to be scheduled per protocol at end of cycle 3 (approximately 2 weeks from today).
   - Patient education reinforced: review of signs/symptoms to call the research nurse line immediately (fever, new neurological symptoms, significant dyspnea, chest pain, rash, new headaches, visual changes).

2. Infusion-related reaction: Grade 1, resolved. Pre-medications (diphenhydramine 25 mg IV, acetaminophen 650 mg PO, methylprednisolone 40 mg IV) to be administered prior to all subsequent infusions per updated protocol order. Nursing to monitor infusion rate closely for the first 30 minutes of each subsequent administration. Epinephrine and emergency supplies on-hand per standard protocol.

3. Brain metastases — post-SRS monitoring: Headaches have significantly improved. Neurological examination is without focal deficits. The right frontal lesion treated 3 weeks prior to Cycle 1 Day 1 with 18 Gy x 1 will be re-evaluated on the restaging MRI brain at the end of cycle 3. Given prior radiation necrosis vs. recurrence diagnostic workup, we will carefully evaluate for treatment effect versus further progression on next MRI. Neurosurgery and radiation oncology have been notified of the patient's clinical trial enrollment. If the right frontal lesion continues to grow or if new lesions appear, we will hold a multidisciplinary discussion before any further intervention.
   - Dexamethasone taper: Confirmed complete. No steroid-related complications noted. Blood glucose will continue to be monitored given diabetes.
   - Neurology: Continue duloxetine 60 mg daily for peripheral neuropathy; stable grade 2 neuropathy at this time.

4. Fatigue/nutrition: Grade 1 fatigue and approximately 1.5 kg unintentional weight loss. Referral to oncology dietitian placed today. Will recommend nutritional supplementation (Ensure or equivalent, 1-2 cans daily as tolerated). If appetite does not improve, consider low-dose mirtazapine or megestrol for appetite stimulation at next visit. Encourage light physical activity as tolerated to preserve functional status and counteract deconditioning.

5. Anemia: Grade 1, stable at Hgb 10.8 g/dL. No transfusion indicated at this time. Continue to monitor CBC per protocol. Iron studies were checked at last assessment; no iron deficiency noted at that time. Will recheck ferritin and iron saturation at cycle 2 assessment given ongoing weight loss and reduced oral intake.

6. Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 2, stable from pre-trial baseline; attributed to prior carboplatin. No new agents in current regimen with known neurotoxicity. Continue duloxetine 60 mg daily. Re-evaluate at each visit; if worsening, neurology follow-up to be arranged and protocol-specified dose modification procedures to be reviewed.

7. Cardiovascular: Blood pressure well-controlled (128/76 mmHg today) on amlodipine and lisinopril. QTc 438 ms on today's ECG — within acceptable range; no dose modification needed. Continue QTc monitoring per protocol schedule. Echocardiogram at baseline showed preserved LVEF 62%; next ECHO at approximately cycle 4. Continue current antihypertensives.

8. Diabetes: Metformin continued. Fasting glucose 112 mg/dL today — acceptable. HbA1c ordered today (results pending). Patient has been off corticosteroids for approximately 3 weeks since completion of the post-SRS dexamethasone taper; glucose levels should be improving. Will review HbA1c at next visit.

9. COPD: Respiratory status at baseline (O2 sat 95% on room air). Continue tiotropium and albuterol PRN. No acute exacerbation. Encourage patient to avoid respiratory irritants. Pulmonology follow-up to be arranged for routine COPD monitoring if not already established.

10. Immune-related adverse event surveillance: Baseline thyroid function, liver enzymes, CBC, and metabolic panel all within acceptable range. Patient counseled on symptoms of immune-related adverse events — specifically rash, diarrhea, new cough/dyspnea, joint pain, and jaundice — with instructions to call the research line promptly if any develop. Baseline cortisol and ACTH to be drawn per protocol at cycle 2 (if not yet obtained).

11. Medications: Discussion held to discontinue folic acid and vitamin B12 supplementation that were initiated for pemetrexed-based chemotherapy (now discontinued approximately 2 months ago). Per protocol review, no contraindication to discontinuation; patient advised she may discontinue these supplements. Confirmed she is not taking any herbal supplements or over-the-counter products that would be prohibited per protocol.

12. Psychosocial: Social work follow-up confirmed — patient was seen by the oncology social worker 2 weeks ago. She is engaging with support services. Her daughter Linda continues to be a very present and supportive caregiver. Patient expressed cautious optimism about the trial, appreciating the scientific rationale for targeting both EGFR and MET. She asked about the likelihood of response; I explained that it is genuinely too early to know, but that the molecular rationale for this regimen in her specific genomic context (EGFR L858R + MET amplification) is well-founded, and that the trial was designed with patients like her specifically in mind. I reinforced that we would have a clearer picture after restaging in 2 weeks.

13. Clinical trial compliance: Research coordinator confirmed study drug compliance and protocol adherence for cycle 1. All required assessments completed. Patient's research diary reviewed; she has been diligent in recording symptoms. Next required on-study assessments at cycle 2 day 1 and day 15, and the cycle 3 end-of-cycle restaging.

Return to clinic for Cycle 2, Day 1 in approximately 1 week. Research nurse line reviewed and contact information provided to patient and daughter.

Time spent with patient and family: approximately 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

---

Dictated by: Attending Thoracic Oncologist
Department of Thoracic/Thoracic Oncology
University Comprehensive Cancer Center
